items.title: Agentic AI for Maintainers: Getting the Most From GitHub Copilot Enterprise - Nate Waddington, CNCF
items.author: CNCF [Cloud Native Computing Foundation]
items.content:
<article>Don't miss out! Join us at our next KubeCon + CloudNativeCon events in Mumbai, India (18-19 June, 2026), Yokohama, Japan (29-30 July, 2026), and Shanghai, China (8-9 September, 2026). Connect with our current graduated, incubating, and sandbox projects as the community gathers to further the education and advancement of cloud native computing. Learn more at https://kubecon.io

Agentic AI for Maintainers: Getting the Most From GitHub Copilot Enterprise - Nate Waddington, CNCF

The CNCF has partnered with GitHub to provide Enterprise Copilot for maintainers—but how do you actually use these agentic tools effectively? This session shares practical strategies for leveraging Copilot's full spectrum of agentic capabilities in your maintainer workflows.

We'll move beyond basic code completion to explore agent mode and the coding agent, showing when each makes sense for different tasks. You'll see how to create custom instructions that teach Copilot your project's conventions, and how to use Copilot for cross-repository research and work.

Key topics:
- Agent mode vs. coding agent: when to use each
- Custom instructions (.github/copilot-instructions.md)
- Cross-repo workflows
- Security considerations for open source

Whether you maintain a sandbox or graduated project, leave with practical approaches you can implement immediately.</article>

items.title: Cluster Autoscaler Evolution - Kuba Tużnik, Google & Jack Francis, Microsoft
items.author: CNCF [Cloud Native Computing Foundation]
items.content:
<article>Don't miss out! Join us at our next KubeCon + CloudNativeCon events in Mumbai, India (18-19 June, 2026), Yokohama, Japan (29-30 July, 2026), and Shanghai, China (8-9 September, 2026). Connect with our current graduated, incubating, and sandbox projects as the community gathers to further the education and advancement of cloud native computing. Learn more at https://kubecon.io

Cluster Autoscaler Evolution - Kuba Tużnik, Google & Jack Francis, Microsoft

Cluster Autoscaler automatically provisions new Nodes in response to pending Pods, and consolidates unnecessary Nodes to optimize the cost-effectiveness of a Kubernetes cluster. Cluster Autoscaler is almost as old as Kubernetes itself - v1.0.0 was released in 2017. Kubernetes has changed tremendously since then, and Cluster Autoscaler grew in turn - from a relatively niche use-case, to a fundamental feature for running Kubernetes clusters at scale.

This rapid growth has presented many interesting challenges and Cluster Autoscaler has substantially changed several times along the way. At the same time, some of the key approaches taken in the codebase 8 years ago have survived until today - and now pose a serious maintainability burden. In order to address that, Cluster Autoscaler will go through some major architectural changes in the near future.

The presentation will briefly cover Cluster Autoscaler history, then will go over the planned architectural changes in detail.</article>

items.title: It's 2030. What Does Prow Look Like? - Petr Muller, Red Hat
items.author: CNCF [Cloud Native Computing Foundation]
items.content:
<article>Don't miss out! Join us at our next KubeCon + CloudNativeCon events in Mumbai, India (18-19 June, 2026), Yokohama, Japan (29-30 July, 2026), and Shanghai, China (8-9 September, 2026). Connect with our current graduated, incubating, and sandbox projects as the community gathers to further the education and advancement of cloud native computing. Learn more at https://kubecon.io

It's 2030. What Does Prow Look Like? - Petr Muller, Red Hat

The Kubernetes project and its ecosystem still heavily depend on Prow. It drives workflows, manages code ownership, runs CI, and merges pull requests. Most Kubernetes contributors encounter it at some point.

But Prow was largely built for 2020 Kubernetes. It's 2026 now. GitHub has merge queues. Actions exist. The CI/CD landscape has evolved. Ideas that were experimental in 2020 have matured into boring products today.

In this session, I want to ask important questions in front of Prow's target audience, seeking answers to some and ideas on others. Is Prow still serving Kubernetes contributors well? Where does it excel? Where does it create friction? What should we keep, modernize, or drop?

The goal is to inspire conversations that continue beyond the Summit and help steer Prow where it needs to be in 2030 and beyond.</article>

items.title: Building Secure Package Pipelines - André Silva, LexisNexis Risk Solutions
items.author: CNCF [Cloud Native Computing Foundation]
items.content:
<article>Don't miss out! Join us at our next KubeCon + CloudNativeCon events in Mumbai, India (18-19 June, 2026), Yokohama, Japan (29-30 July, 2026), and Shanghai, China (8-9 September, 2026). Connect with our current graduated, incubating, and sandbox projects as the community gathers to further the education and advancement of cloud native computing. Learn more at https://kubecon.io

Building Secure Package Pipelines - André Silva, LexisNexis Risk Solutions

This session guides you through creating a secure package pipeline that any open-source maintainer can achieve. I will show how we eliminated secrets with OIDC authentication, introduced automated SBOM generation for each release, signed builds with cryptographic attestations and used matrix CI to test across platforms.

We use bots to keep our dependencies up to date, CodeQL to scan our repositories for vulnerabilities and automate releases with Release Please. To increase the security of GitHub Actions, we pin hashes and permissions. These measures reduce risks associated with compromised dependencies, supply chain attacks and manual errors, while also improving compliance and trust for everyone.

By the end, you will have a blueprint for securing your projects. The ecosystem will benefit from improved security practices, increased transparency and better compliance with standards. Contributors can fork, audit and extend projects, knowing the integrity of the build process is assured.</article>

items.title: Guidelines for Achieving Project Release Maturity - Andrew Block, Carol Valencia & Nabarun Pal
items.author: CNCF [Cloud Native Computing Foundation]
items.content:
<article>Don't miss out! Join us at our next KubeCon + CloudNativeCon events in Mumbai, India (18-19 June, 2026), Yokohama, Japan (29-30 July, 2026), and Shanghai, China (8-9 September, 2026). Connect with our current graduated, incubating, and sandbox projects as the community gathers to further the education and advancement of cloud native computing. Learn more at https://kubecon.io

Guidelines for Achieving Project Release Maturity - Andrew Block, Red Hat; Carol Valencia, KrolCloud; Nabarun Pal, Broadcom

The Project Release Guidelines initiative was developed to define clear guidelines and best practices for managing project releases, aligned with CNCF project maturity expectations. It covers key areas such as versioning, branching strategies, release artifacts, security considerations, common governance patterns and communication processes. By providing such guidance, projects can benefit by understanding the signals that indicate a project is ready to cut a release, which release practices are essential versus optional at early stages, and how release processes should evolve as projects mature toward Graduation.

We’ll explore the Project Release Guidelines initiative in depth including facilitating a dynamic discussion with attendees and show how mature CNCF projects, like Kubernetes and Helm, align to these principles and can be used as a model to implement these approaches within your own projects.</article>

items.title: What We Learned After Mentoring 30+ Mentees - Arthur Sens, Grafana Labs & Bartłomiej Płotka, Google
items.author: CNCF [Cloud Native Computing Foundation]
items.content:
<article>Don't miss out! Join us at our next KubeCon + CloudNativeCon events in Mumbai, India (18-19 June, 2026), Yokohama, Japan (29-30 July, 2026), and Shanghai, China (8-9 September, 2026). Connect with our current graduated, incubating, and sandbox projects as the community gathers to further the education and advancement of cloud native computing. Learn more at https://kubecon.io

What We Learned After Mentoring 30+ Mentees - Arthur Sens, Grafana Labs & Bartłomiej Płotka, Google

When we signed up to mentor our first CNCF mentees, we were nervous. How do you pick the right people? How do you handle dozens—or hundreds—of candidates messaging you from every channel imaginable? How do you set expectations without scaring people away? And once the mentorship begins… how do you actually help someone thrive in open source?

Across more than 30 CNCF (LFX, GSoC) mentorships, we made mistakes, learned patterns, refined our processes, and discovered what matters for mentees and, more importantly, for us. In this talk, we’ll share the lessons that have changed how we approach mentorship: selecting the right co-mentors, finding what motivates you to mentor, evaluating applications, designing clear interviews, and nurturing a sense of belonging, ultimately empowering contributors to stay engaged long after the program ends.

If you’ve ever felt demotivated or unprepared to mentor, we’ve been there—and there’s a clearer path than you think.</article>

Ask the Experts: Kubernetes Steering Committee - Kat Cosgrove, Minimus; Maciej Szulik, Defense Unicorns; Antonio Ojea, Google

Join us for another interactive Q&A with representatives from the Kubernetes Steering Committee! We’ll talk about governance, the future of Kubernetes, and anything else you can think of, so bring your questions! A form will be provided to ask questions anonymously.</article>

items.title: The Practices That Made Me Go 'Wow': What Every CNCF Project Ca... Rohit Agrawal & Kateryna Nezdolii
items.author: CNCF [Cloud Native Computing Foundation]
items.content:
<article>Don't miss out! Join us at our next KubeCon + CloudNativeCon events in Mumbai, India (18-19 June, 2026), Yokohama, Japan (29-30 July, 2026), and Shanghai, China (8-9 September, 2026). Connect with our current graduated, incubating, and sandbox projects as the community gathers to further the education and advancement of cloud native computing. Learn more at https://kubecon.io

The Practices That Made Me Go 'Wow': What Every CNCF Project Can Learn From Envoy - Rohit Agrawal, Databricks & Kateryna Nezdolii, Isovalent

When we became Envoy maintainers, we expected a codebase. What we found was literally a masterclass in sustainable open source engineering. From an automated system to track deprecation and tags PR authors two release later, to CI pipelines running all the builds including memory & address sanitizers and continuous fuzzing via OSS-Fuzz, Envoy has institutionalized practices that prevent the entropy every large project faces.

This talk shares the practices that genuinely surprised me as a new maintainer. Every practice we'll cover is adoptable by any project including the runtime feature guards with built-in deprecation timelines, automated GitHub issue creation for cleanup tasks, test ownership models that scale, and CI health strategies that keep a 2M+ line codebase green. Moreover the policies around reviews, code ownership, etc. are beyond impressive.

You'll leave with concrete practices to reduce maintainer burden, improve backward compatibility, no matter your project's size.</article>

items.title: Guide Us on Next Steps in Kubernetes Device Manage... John B, Janet K, Marlow W, Patrick O & Kevin K
items.author: CNCF [Cloud Native Computing Foundation]
items.content:
<article>Don't miss out! Join us at our next KubeCon + CloudNativeCon events in Mumbai, India (18-19 June, 2026), Yokohama, Japan (29-30 July, 2026), and Shanghai, China (8-9 September, 2026). Connect with our current graduated, incubating, and sandbox projects as the community gathers to further the education and advancement of cloud native computing. Learn more at https://kubecon.io

Guide Us on Next Steps in Kubernetes Device Management - John Belamaric & Janet Kuo, Google; Marlow Warnicke (Weston), SchedMD LLC; Patrick Ohly, Intel; Kevin Klues, Nvidia GmbH


Kubernetes Working Group Device Management has been hard at work making Kubernetes understand hardware better.

This session is your chance, as community members and CNCF ecosystem project maintainers, to help us figure out what more you need from the Kubernetes platform for device management.

We'll start the discussion with what we've done and are already planning for device management, like Dynamic Resource Allocation (DRA). But then will ask you for your requirements and ideas. Some possibilities:
- Device health management
- Failure handling
- Interconnected, multi-node device allocation
- In-place addition of and/or reconfiguration of devices
- What should be optional and what should be part of AI conformance
- Your requirements and ideas!

Come join the WG leads and contributors and guide our plans for the next few releases!</article>

items.title: Keynote: Building AI in the Open: How the Cloud Native Community Sha... Moderated by: Mario Fahlandt
items.author: CNCF [Cloud Native Computing Foundation]
items.content:
<article>Don't miss out! Join us at our next KubeCon + CloudNativeCon events in Mumbai, India (18-19 June, 2026), Yokohama, Japan (29-30 July, 2026), and Shanghai, China (8-9 September, 2026). Connect with our current graduated, incubating, and sandbox projects as the community gathers to further the education and advancement of cloud native computing. Learn more at https://kubecon.io

Keynote: Building AI in the Open: How the Cloud Native Community Shapes What Comes Next - Moderated by: Mario Fahlandt, Customer Delivery Architect, Kubermatic

AI's rapid rise, sparked by the breakthrough of LLMs and tools like ChatGPT, was anything but subtle. Yet what makes this moment truly different is not the hype, but the transition from prompt driven models to agent based runtimes. This shift fundamentally expands what AI can do in real world, production environments and crucially, how organizations can realize measurable ROI. From modern code generation assistants to autonomous workflows, AI is no longer experimental - it is operational.

So what does this mean for the cloud native ecosystem and open source communities like CNCF and KubeCon? Quite simply, it raises the bar. Supporting AI at scale requires moving beyond request-centric inference toward workload-aware, distributed inference systems - systems that are cloud native by design and open by default.

This keynote focuses on AI-driven community innovation. Mario will highlight six community led projects that demonstrate how open source and cloud native principles are shaping the future of AI infrastructure and platforms. He will then hand over to Ricardo to moderate a 10 minute panel on "How End Users are Adopting AI", exploring real challenges and opportunities facing practitioners today.

The goal is not just to inform, but to spark ongoing conversation. We invite you to continue engaging with the AI discussion throughout the summit and beyond - because the next phase of AI will be built not by a single vendor, but by a vibrant, collaborative community.</article>

items.title: Does Your Project Want a UI in Kubernetes-SIGs/headlamp? - René Dudfield & Santhosh Nagaraj
items.author: CNCF [Cloud Native Computing Foundation]
items.content:
<article>Don't miss out! Join us at our next KubeCon + CloudNativeCon events in Mumbai, India (18-19 June, 2026), Yokohama, Japan (29-30 July, 2026), and Shanghai, China (8-9 September, 2026). Connect with our current graduated, incubating, and sandbox projects as the community gathers to further the education and advancement of cloud native computing. Learn more at https://kubecon.io

Does Your Project Want a UI in Kubernetes-SIGs/headlamp? - René Dudfield & Santhosh Nagaraj, Microsoft

Headlamp is now part of the Kubernetes project. Our goal is to become a pretty formidable UI for Kubernetes and the CNCF ecosystem. One of the best parts is that it can be extended to support Kubernetes extensions, especially other CNCF projects.

Headlamp UIs already exist for dozens of Kubernetes extensions like cert-manager, gateway-API, Karpenter, Kubescape, Gate Keeper, KEDA, and more.

Do you want to provide your expertise for your CNCF project so it to has a UI available for Headlamp?

We will show what Headlamp can do. We want to UI all the things, but need to learn from people who understand the things.</article>

items.title: Building Bridges: How the OTel End User SIG Helps Foster Co... Adriana Villela, Josh Lee & Reese Lee
items.author: CNCF [Cloud Native Computing Foundation]
items.content:
<article>Don't miss out! Join us at our next KubeCon + CloudNativeCon events in Mumbai, India (18-19 June, 2026), Yokohama, Japan (29-30 July, 2026), and Shanghai, China (8-9 September, 2026). Connect with our current graduated, incubating, and sandbox projects as the community gathers to further the education and advancement of cloud native computing. Learn more at https://kubecon.io

Building Bridges: How the OTel End User SIG Helps Foster Community - Adriana Villela, Dynatrace; Josh Lee, Altinity; Reese Lee, New Relic

One of the core parts of any open source project is community – after all, you need people to write the code, and people to use the code. However, you also need people to: raise awareness about the project, drive the adoption and implementation of the project, and to connect these intersecting groups.

There are, of course, many ways to do this and build a thriving project community. In this session, learn about supporting and helping build the OpenTelemetry (OTel) community through the lens of End User SIG maintainers/Open Source community managers. Learn:
* Brief history of OTel
* Brief history of the End User SIG
* SIG initiatives
* How forces of entropy can be derailing, and how to mitigate it
* Strategies for building & sustaining community

Attendees will leave equipped with strategies they can implement today to build and grow their open source communities.</article>

items.title: Running the Runners: How CNCF Powers GitHub Actions at Scale - Koray Oksay, Kubermatic
items.author: CNCF [Cloud Native Computing Foundation]
items.content:
<article>Don't miss out! Join us at our next KubeCon + CloudNativeCon events in Mumbai, India (18-19 June, 2026), Yokohama, Japan (29-30 July, 2026), and Shanghai, China (8-9 September, 2026). Connect with our current graduated, incubating, and sandbox projects as the community gathers to further the education and advancement of cloud native computing. Learn more at https://kubecon.io

Running the Runners: How CNCF Powers GitHub Actions at Scale - Koray Oksay, Kubermatic

Continuous Integration is the backbone of every CNCF project, but large GitHub Action runners can quickly become a major cost burden. To help projects build, test, and release efficiently, we created a shared hosted runner platform, powered by Oracle Cloud and donated infrastructure credits.

In this session, we’ll take you behind the scenes of how this platform was built and operated: how it scales runners on demand, isolates workloads across projects, and integrates seamlessly with GitHub Actions. We’ll also cover lessons learned around cost optimization, security, and community sustainability.

If you maintain or contribute to a CNCF project, this is your backstage pass to understanding how your CI is powered and how you can make the most of it.</article>
